title: Sebastien Raine

jokes.text: Moira announces Sebastien Raine is coming; David reveals he's his ex
jokes.setup: Moira enthusiastically announces the 'extraordinary' photographer Sebastien Raine is coming to collaborate with her, framing it as thrilling professional news
jokes.punchline: David flatly reveals 'You mean my ex?' — instantly deflating the grandeur and recontextualizing the situation as deeply personal and awkward
jokes.explanation: A strong cold-open reversal. Moira's breathless reverence for 'Sebastien Raine' is immediately punctured by David's three-word reveal. The joke works because it's entirely character-specific — only David would be this person's ex, and only Moira would be this oblivious to the social landmine she's stepping on. Well-structured with great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: When did you date Sebastien?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We dated for about two years.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, he was seeing other people during that time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I'm counting the months where we were *together* together.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Which months are those?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: All of them.

jokes.text: You mean my ex? / When did you date Sebastien Raine?
jokes.setup: Moira asks if David is aware of the extraordinary photographer Sebastien Raine, treating him as a grand cultural figure.
jokes.punchline: David immediately deflates the grandeur by revealing he dated him, shocking Moira.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection — Moira builds up Sebastien as a lofty cultural figure, David punctures it instantly with personal history. Works because David's romantic disasters are already established character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Sebastien Raine is a visionary. He's a creative genius. He's a man who sees the world differently than the rest of us, and that is both a gift and a curse. And yes, he may have publicly humiliated me in front of my peers, destroyed my professional reputation, and made me question everything I know about myself as a merchant, but that doesn't change the fact that he is a true artist.

jokes.text: They dated for like a month and a half and David got very upset about it. / It was almost three months. Four, if you include the month that he was seeing other people.
jokes.setup: Alexis dismissively summarizes the relationship as 'a month and a half.'
jokes.punchline: David corrects her with increasingly pathetic precision — 'almost three months. Four, if you include the month he was seeing other people' — which only makes it worse for him.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed escalating self-own. David trying to claim dignity by extending the timeline actually highlights how undignified the situation was — he's counting a month where Sebastien was openly seeing others. The structure is precise: correction #1 sounds reasonable, correction #2 reveals the pathos. Pure character comedy; only David Rose would make this argument.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, if you're going to bring that up, I should clarify the timeline. It was after we broke up.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, we didn't break up. You just... didn't tell me you were seeing someone else.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, semantically—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You made it worse. You made it so much worse.

jokes.text: And you met him through me. / That's nonsense. I met Sebastien Raine at an art opening years ago. / At my gallery.
jokes.setup: David insists he met Sebastien independently, not through Moira.
jokes.punchline: Moira one-words him: 'At my gallery.' David's claim of independence is instantly demolished.
jokes.explanation: Economy is the joke's strength — 'At my gallery' does enormous work in two words. Moira doesn't belabor it; she just drops the mic. Very well-structured for such a small moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He literally sat in the food court for six hours eating pretzels and crying.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was not crying. I was eating pretzels. Two separate activities.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, your face was wet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was pretzel salt.

jokes.text: And can you please stop saying his full name. He's a monster, who uses people, and leaves them for dead.
jokes.setup: The family keeps ceremoniously saying 'Sebastien Raine' (full name) with reverence.
jokes.punchline: David's theatrical condemnation ('monster, uses people, leaves them for dead') is hilariously melodramatic for what amounts to a bad breakup.
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose hyperbole — his tendency to catastrophize personal slights into operatic tragedy. The 'leaves them for dead' escalation is very him. The full-name complaint also plays against the rest of the family's pomposity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you don't care, then I have no choice but to go ahead with it. Your apathy has left me no alternative.

jokes.text: I hate to play contrarian, David, but The Times and I, both consider Sebastien Raine a dear friend.
jokes.setup: David is condemning Sebastien as a monster.
jokes.punchline: Moira casually aligns herself with The New York Times as a peer — as if her opinion and the Times' are equivalent institutional voices.
jokes.explanation: Moira's delusion of grandeur is perfectly captured — 'The Times and I' positions her as an institution on par with the paper of record. Solid character work that's very specific to her.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He's got this sort of rugged, unkempt look. Very artisanal.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So handsome in a homeless-y way?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I wouldn't have phrased it that way, but... yes.

jokes.text: Remember when he dumped you? And you ate all those mall pretzels, and watched 'Bridget Jones's Diary' every day for a year.
jokes.setup: The family is debating Sebastien Raine's merits.
jokes.punchline: Alexis gleefully weaponizes the specific humiliating details of David's post-breakup behavior — mall pretzels and daily Bridget Jones rewatches.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'mall pretzels' makes this exceptional — it's not just sad food, it's specifically mall pretzels, which carries a particular aura of desperate, tasteless comfort-eating for someone of David's former socioeconomic status. The Bridget Jones detail compounds it perfectly. Very quotable and fully earned through character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is he here? Can you see him?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's pulling up now. Oh, he's getting out of the car.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And? What's he doing?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's walking. He's still walking. He's at the door.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is it. This is happening.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's reaching for the handle. His hand is on the handle. He's opening it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's inside. He's looking around. He's... he's leaving.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What do you mean he's leaving?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He took one look and left. He's gone.

jokes.text: It wasn't a whole year. And I will not feel shame about the mall pretzels.
jokes.setup: Alexis mocks David for his post-breakup wallowing.
jokes.punchline: David only disputes the duration, completely conceding the embarrassing behavior itself — and then defiantly defends the mall pretzels.
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: David's protest only corrects the timeline (implicitly admitting the behavior), then pivots to unexpected, genuine pride about the pretzels. 'I will not feel shame about the mall pretzels' is highly quotable and a near-perfect David Rose line — self-awareness wrapped in defiance wrapped in absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: Well, this is... cozy. Very... economical. I appreciate how you've embraced the aesthetic of 'we've given up.'

jokes.text: Okay, that's enough news for today. [David starts to leave] Ummm no, what about him?
jokes.setup: David, overwhelmed, declares 'that's enough news' and tries to exit the conversation.
jokes.punchline: Moira hasn't even delivered the actual news yet and stops him — he tried to flee before the bomb dropped.
jokes.explanation: A functional structural beat — David's escape instinct is well-established, and the reveal that the main news hasn't even arrived yet is a nice comedic setup for what follows.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: David, you look... healthy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...Thank you?

jokes.text: He's coming here. / When flying me to New York would be a lot easier. And a welcome escape.
jokes.setup: Moira announces Sebastien is coming to Schitt's Creek to collaborate.
jokes.punchline: David's reaction is ostensibly practical ('easier to fly me to New York') but the real joke is 'a welcome escape' — revealing his dread of both Sebastien and the town.
jokes.explanation: Efficient double-meaning joke — David covers personal dread with faux-practical reasoning. Works as character shorthand for his ongoing discomfort with the town.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: You know, what you're doing here is really very brave.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you?

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: Living like this, in these... conditions. It's quite brave.

jokes.text: I could cancel, but you just told me you don't care so now I have no choice but to meet him.
jokes.setup: David says he doesn't care what Moira does about Sebastien.
jokes.punchline: Moira uses David's feigned indifference as ironclad justification to do exactly what she wanted — a masterclass in Moira-logic.
jokes.explanation: Moira's twisted logic is perfectly rendered — she takes David's dismissal and converts it into permission, then obligation. This is specific Moira behavior that rewards familiarity with the character.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: So you must be David's girlfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, I'm his landlord.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: Oh, same thing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. No, it's really not.

jokes.text: He's most likely wearing a very expensive sweater, that doesn't look very expensive.
jokes.setup: Stevie is about to describe the approaching figure.
jokes.punchline: David pre-identifies Sebastien through this perfectly specific sartorial tell — expensive-looking poverty-chic.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical character description. 'A very expensive sweater that doesn't look very expensive' captures an entire cultural archetype (the pretentious art-world poverty-cosplay aesthetic) in one precise, contradictory phrase. Only David, as someone deeply fluent in that world, could nail this. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: So what do you say? Want to get out of here and go back to my place?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay?!

jokes.text: Is he like, really... Really handsome in a homeless-y way sort of way? Yes. / Yup. Then he is coming here.
jokes.setup: Stevie is trying to identify Sebastien from the window.
jokes.punchline: Her description — 'really handsome in a homeless-y way' — is confirmed as a sufficient and complete positive ID.
jokes.explanation: 'Handsome in a homeless-y way' is a funny, specific phrase that works as both joke and character description. The comedic beat is that this is apparently a recognized type David knows well enough to confirm as a fingerprint. Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: He's walking. He's walking towards here. And now he's coming in. Now he's coming in... and so he's here. He's here.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie nervously narrate Sebastien's approach in real time.
jokes.punchline: The overlapping, anxious play-by-play commentary collapses into breathless obviousness ('He's here') — the joke is that neither of them knows what to do.
jokes.explanation: A well-observed comedic beat — the mutual nervous narration reveals both characters' anxiety without stating it. The humor is in the redundancy building to the flat 'He's here.'

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: This cafe is so... unassertive. It's almost as if the lack of ambition is intentional. A bold statement about restraint.

jokes.text: I can't believe this is where you live. [beat] I think you're brave.
jokes.setup: Sebastien enters the motel and meets David.
jokes.punchline: He follows up what is clearly a cutting assessment of the motel ('I can't believe this is where you live') with 'I think you're brave' — using faux-compliment to twist the knife with maximum condescension.
jokes.explanation: The pause between condemnation and faint praise is the entire joke. 'I think you're brave' lands perfectly as the cruelest possible upgrade from 'I can't believe this is where you live.' Economical, perfectly timed, and establishing Sebastien's character instantly.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: Creativity requires movement. The body must be in motion for the mind to truly create.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'll have a medium oat milk latte with an extra shot, please.

jokes.text: Look at you... You look really... Healthy.
jokes.setup: Sebastien greets David warmly after seeing him for the first time since their breakup
jokes.punchline: 'Healthy' — the most damning possible compliment, implying David has let himself go or settled into small-town life
jokes.explanation: Masterclass in economy and implication. The pause before 'Healthy' does all the work — the audience fills in all the worse things he almost said. In fashion/art-world parlance, 'healthy' is code for 'you've gained weight and lost your edge.' The cringe of David's 'Thanks' in response is palpable. Approaches the 8.0 anchor quality through sheer economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I should be able to claim these as volunteer hours because I'm not getting paid.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, you literally just said you have a paid job.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, but I'm not being paid for the volunteering part.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: I like your sweater. / You must be David's girlfriend. / No... / No, I own The Motel, so that makes me more like your landlord? / No. It doesn't.
jokes.setup: Sebastien mistakes Stevie for David's girlfriend after complimenting her sweater.
jokes.punchline: Stevie attempts to reframe herself as his 'landlord' which David immediately and definitively shuts down ('No. It doesn't.'), while the sweater compliment mirrors David's earlier description of Sebastien's own wardrobe.
jokes.explanation: Multiple layers: the sweater callback is subtle, Stevie's landlord gambit is absurd, and David's flat correction is perfect. The 'No. It doesn't.' is ideally economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have to do community service. It's court-ordered.

jokes.text: I'd love to Polaroid you naked one day. / Okay. / Okay?!
jokes.setup: Sebastien is being introduced to Stevie when he casually proposes photographing her nude.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's breezy 'Okay' is the joke — she accepts without hesitation, which shocks David more than the proposition itself.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure that fires perfectly: Sebastien's outrageous proposition → Stevie's unfazed acceptance → David's incredulous 'Okay?!' The comedy is in the inversion — David, who knows Sebastien, is horrified; Stevie, who just met him, is fine. Pure character reveal for both Stevie and David. Very well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know if I'm really the right person to be teaching seniors how to dance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, what if they try to touch me?

jokes.text: Yeah, I just feel like we have unfinished business, you and I. / Room five is available. / Room five sounds great.
jokes.setup: Sebastien tells David they have 'unfinished business,' implying romantic interest in rekindling.
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately books him a room (facilitating David's obvious discomfort), and Sebastien accepts with zero awkwardness — the cringe is David standing there as this unfolds.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's pragmatic facilitation of David's nightmare situation is funny because she's simultaneously helping Sebastien and torturing David. The ease with which this goes from 'unfinished business' to 'Room five sounds great' is efficiently uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Just act natural.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I am acting natural.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, you're not. You're doing that thing where you're being all stiff and weird.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not being stiff and weird.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You absolutely are. You're like a robot that just learned how to talk.

jokes.text: So brave. / Hm-hm. / So brave. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Sebastien has called David 'brave' for living at the motel.
jokes.punchline: He repeats it again as he leaves — 'So brave' — turning his condescending praise into a recurring taunt, and David's 'Okay' is perfect defeated resignation.
jokes.explanation: The repetition elevates the callback into a running gag. Sebastien weaponizes the word with such ease and David's quiet 'Okay' is resigned and perfect. The show clearly knows this word is now loaded and deploys it efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny, I need you to cover for me. Tell Jazzagal I was with you last night.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What do you want me to say we were doing?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I don't know, something boring. Say we went to get a haircut.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A haircut? At night?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, a 9 PM haircut appointment. Very exclusive. The barber only sees clients after dark.

jokes.text: I love it. It's just so unassertive. You can't find this in New York City.
jokes.setup: Sebastien is taken to the local café/bar.
jokes.punchline: His 'compliment' — 'so unassertive' — is a devastating art-world put-down disguised as praise, delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Sebastien's character is crystallized here — he aestheticizes poverty and mediocrity as an artistic gesture. 'Unassertive' as a compliment is perfectly chosen, conveying the condescending art-world gaze on 'authentic' small-town life.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I promised I'd help you stay away from poker tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's right, John. I need to focus on my family.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: We're starting a game in the back room if you want in.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm in.

jokes.text: New York misses you, Moira. / You're too kind. / And correct.
jokes.setup: Sebastien flatters Moira about New York missing her.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'You're too kind. And correct.' — accepting the flattery and then upgrading it to objective fact.
jokes.explanation: 'You're too kind. And correct.' is one of the episode's most quotable lines. The structure is immaculate: the self-deprecating start ('too kind') pivots instantly to total self-belief ('correct'). This is quintessentially Moira — modesty as a launching pad for self-aggrandizement. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny, I've been running this scheme for three weeks and you haven't noticed a thing!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What scheme?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly!

jokes.text: I was touring with Madonna. She commissioned a series of anti-portraits.
jokes.setup: Sebastien is telling Moira what he's been up to.
jokes.punchline: 'Anti-portraits' — a pretentious art-world term deployed with complete seriousness that simultaneously name-drops Madonna and invents a baffling concept.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in 'anti-portraits' — the word is meaningless but sounds exactly like pretentious art-world speak. Works as character establishment for Sebastien.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A motel? Sebastien, that's hardly the behavior of a man who frequents cafés.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: It's just a room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's renegade behavior, darling. That's what it is.

jokes.text: Creativity lives on its feet. Anything else is bullshit.
jokes.setup: Sebastien suggests they walk to discuss the project.
jokes.punchline: The faux-aphorism 'Creativity lives on its feet' is delivered as profound wisdom, then the blunt 'Anything else is bullshit' punctures its own pomposity.
jokes.explanation: Good Sebastien character work — he speaks in performative aphorisms. The 'bullshit' ending is unexpected enough to land.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course they have a room. People recognize us.

jokes.text: I just ordered you a Sanka-ccino.
jokes.setup: Sebastien dramatically announces they must walk immediately.
jokes.punchline: Moira tries to preserve the social niceties of the visit by noting she ordered him a 'Sanka-ccino' — a portmanteau combining the discount instant coffee brand Sanka with 'cappuccino,' revealing the café's aspirational-but-limited menu.
jokes.explanation: 'Sanka-ccino' is a quietly brilliant piece of writing — one invented word that communicates the entire joke: small-town café trying to be cosmopolitan, Moira trying to be a gracious host in reduced circumstances, and the gap between her world and her current reality. High originality for such a tiny moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait. He got a room? Just like that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How did he get a room so easily?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This wasn't supposed to be an overnight visit.

jokes.text: Um nothing. It just says that I work here. / Uh-huh. This is for volunteer hours. But you don't volunteer here. You work here. / But, I volunteer to work here.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks Ted to sign a school form claiming she volunteers at the clinic.
jokes.punchline: Her defense — 'I volunteer to work here' — is logically incoherent but delivered with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's logical contortion is very in-character — she genuinely believes her sophistry is persuasive. The 'I volunteer to work here' line is a good comedic argument that holds up precisely long enough to seem almost reasonable before collapsing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not camera-ready. I haven't had my morning coffee, I haven't done my hair, I haven't applied my makeup, I haven't chosen my outfit, and frankly, I'm not in the headspace to be memorialized in this moment.

jokes.text: Okay. It's just, I thought my community service hours would apply but they don't count because they're court-ordered. So...
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to find volunteer hours to graduate.
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals her community service hours are court-ordered — a criminal history disclosed as a bureaucratic inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Alexis characterization — the dark detail of court-ordered community service is dropped with zero drama, as if it's the most natural thing in the world. The 'So...' trailing off is perfect. High character integration because this reveals Alexis's entire history in a throwaway line.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: It's terrifying and important. Like a Diane Arbus photograph, but with more... corn.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: The desolation is almost transcendent. One could weep at the sheer banality of the pastoral.

jokes.text: Well, tonight's gonna be fun though, dance lessons! / We'd have to touch them.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions dancing with seniors as an option for volunteer hours.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's concern — 'We'd have to touch them' — reveals her squeamishness about elderly people as her primary obstacle to volunteering.
jokes.explanation: The delay in Alexis's objection — Ted says it'll be fun, she processes for a beat, then lands on 'We'd have to touch them' — is the structure that makes it work. Her real concern (physical contact with old people) is funnier than any principled objection.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: I need you to connect with the field. Feel the earth beneath you. You are not just standing in a field—you are becoming the field.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: The field, Moira. Become one with it. Let it consume you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I think what's happening here is that you've confused directing with a spiritual awakening, and I'm not interested in either.

jokes.text: Now act like you're talking to me. / I am talking to you. / No. Not like that. It's got to be more conversational. You know, like, um... 'Yes the weather is cold.' / Okay, I'm not following.
jokes.setup: Roland wants Johnny to pretend to be in casual conversation to avoid Bob's poker invitation.
jokes.punchline: His example of casual conversation — 'Yes the weather is cold' — is so stilted and odd that it undermines his own scheme. Johnny's 'I'm not following' is the straight-man beat.
jokes.explanation: 'Yes the weather is cold' as Roland's model of natural conversation is absurdly bad. The gap between Roland's confidence and the quality of his plan is the ongoing joke of the Roland/Johnny dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: He took some picturettes.

jokes.text: I wouldn't mind playing, Bob. / Really? / Well... / That's great!
jokes.setup: Roland has engineered a cover story to avoid Bob's poker night and asked Johnny to back him up.
jokes.punchline: The moment Bob appears, Johnny immediately accepts the invitation, completely betraying Roland's scheme.
jokes.explanation: The betrayal is telegraphed but executed cleanly. Johnny's instant capitulation reveals that he has no loyalty to Roland's scheme and probably wanted to play poker all along. Good situational comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you can't just pose for every photograph. You need to think about your image.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, perhaps if you spent less time criticizing and more time leaning in, you'd understand the art of the photograph.

jokes.text: Can I ask you something? Um, do you have even the slightest concept of what just happened here?
jokes.setup: Johnny has just blown Roland's entire plan by agreeing to poker night.
jokes.punchline: Roland's exasperated non-question ('do you have even the slightest concept of what just happened here?') is the punctuation on the scene's chaos.
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat that closes the poker-plan scene. Not exceptional but serves the scene's comedic rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Dot apparently brought cleaning supplies to dance class.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so Dot.

jokes.text: There's something very cathartic about stepping into someone else's world. That's one of the reasons we chose to settle here.
jokes.setup: Moira is walking with Sebastien and making conversation about the town.
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes the family's humiliating forced relocation to Schitt's Creek as a deliberate, enlightened lifestyle choice — 'we chose to settle here.'
jokes.explanation: A signature Moira move — rewriting reality to protect her image. 'We chose to settle here' is revisionist history delivered with complete conviction. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There's like, barely any of them left.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, they're seniors, so...

jokes.text: You got yourself a room? Oh, aren't you lucky! They're usually booked up. You must have used our name.
jokes.setup: Moira discovers Sebastien got a room at the motel (which is almost never fully booked).
jokes.punchline: Moira insists the motel is 'usually booked up' and that he must have used the Rose name to secure a room — a complete fiction that preserves her dignity.
jokes.explanation: Another Moira self-deception moment, and it's very well-observed. The Rose Motel being 'usually booked up' is patently absurd, and Moira fully commits. The name-dropping of her own name as a power move in a town where it means nothing is perfectly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, they don't smell like old people! They smell like... normal!

jokes.text: Sorry, it's the way the light is catching you. The photo took itself. / Ah, but you had to push the button.
jokes.setup: Sebastien starts photographing Moira without permission, offering the romantic excuse that 'the photo took itself.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's sharp, perfectly logical rejoinder: 'Ah, but you had to push the button.'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best exchanges. Sebastien deploys a classic pretentious-artist line and Moira — no fool, whatever her vanities — cuts right through it with perfect Wildean economy. 'But you had to push the button' is devastating because it's completely true. The line lands harder because Moira is both charmed AND clear-eyed about the manipulation. Strong craft across the board.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is she giving me the finger?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, she is.

jokes.text: This is my talk now, shoot later look. So, this visage is off-limits for-for the moment.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to set terms for the photography session.
jokes.punchline: She refers to her facial expression as a 'look' with a name ('talk now, shoot later') and refers to her face as 'this visage' — the grandiosity of the vocabulary vs. the mundanity of the request.
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical vocabulary ('this visage,' 'talk now shoot later look') is character comedy at its most consistent. The naming of her own face as if it's a trademarked product is very specific to her.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is Dot's arthritis acting up?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, she's just giving you the finger.

jokes.text: Don't worry, I'm just using you for scale. It's this backdrop. There's a provincial romanticism to all this, that's just... terrifying and important.
jokes.setup: Moira protests being photographed; Sebastien reassures her.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm just using you for scale' — casually dismissing Moira as a prop, followed by describing Schitt's Creek as 'terrifying and important,' which is comically excessive.
jokes.explanation: The casual demotion of Moira to 'scale' is a good insight into Sebastien's character. The 'terrifying and important' description of a small Canadian town is perfectly inflated art-speak.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Roland, you lost again.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Lost? I had a great hand! I had two pairs!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, two pair doesn't beat a straight.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't know we were keeping score.

jokes.text: Well, why don't you actually stand over there? / Where? / Further into the field. / Oh... / Yes! / No, into the field. / Further! / Huh? / Yeah, into the field. / Umm? / Yes. Further! / Sebastien, I was... / Let the field reveal itself to you.
jokes.setup: Sebastien asks Moira to position herself for a photo, ostensibly for the backdrop.
jokes.punchline: He keeps directing her further and further into a field, the directions becoming increasingly absurd ('Let the field reveal itself to you'), suggesting he just wants her out of the frame entirely.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation sequence. The joke structure is progressive — each 'Further!' extends the absurdity. The visual of Moira walking ever deeper into a field while Sebastien photographs the backdrop (without her) peaks with 'Let the field reveal itself to you,' which is the perfect absurdist artist-speak capstone. Strong structure and escalating impact.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I've been playing poker for thirty years. I know all the hands.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh really? Name them.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, there's a Royal Flush, a Straight Flush, Four of a Kind, Full House, Flush, Straight, Three of a Kind, Two Pair, One Pair, and High Card.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's actually correct.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know. And the best part is, I always play with my money on top of my cards so nobody can see what I have.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not... Roland, that defeats the entire purpose.

jokes.text: Okay. As long as you can still hear me. I was also thinking Berlin...
jokes.setup: Moira is far out in a field being directed further away.
jokes.punchline: She continues trying to pitch shoot locations (Berlin, Mr. Schnabel's Palazzo) from deep in a field, shouting to be heard — total commitment to her creative agenda despite physical absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The image of Moira shouting about Berlin from a field is a strong visual-comedy payoff. Her complete obliviousness to how she's being used is tragic-comic. Good visual gag inferred from context.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Bob, running a laundromat is a lot like my business ventures. We're both taking people to the cleaners!

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Roland, that's not... that's literally what a laundromat does.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly! We're the same, you and I.

jokes.text: So he photographed you today then? / Hm-hm. You know just a few dozen snaps about town... picturettes, really.
jokes.setup: Moira has claimed the day was 'about discussing theme and tone, mise en scène.'
jokes.punchline: David cuts to: 'So he photographed you today then?' forcing Moira to admit 'a few dozen snaps' (then downgraded to 'picturettes'), revealing she was already being used.
jokes.explanation: 'Picturettes' is a wonderful Moira-ism — a diminutive that tries to minimize the number of photos while somehow sounding even grander. David's knowing one-question interrogation is very effective.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I lost my watch to gambling.

jokes.text: Why would you have allowed this to happen? / I don't know he just started shooting and shouting and I did what he told me to do and I 'leaned in' to the moment. / How did that work out for you? / I leaned in! I don't know, David. Why don't you try it... sometime!
jokes.setup: David questions how Moira let Sebastien steamroll her into a photo shoot.
jokes.punchline: Moira's defensive invocation of 'leaning in' as her strategy, followed by her doubling down on it even as it clearly went badly, turning the millennial self-help buzzword into a Moira defense mechanism.
jokes.explanation: The 'lean in' joke works on multiple levels: it's funny because Moira is using Sheryl Sandberg jargon, funnier because she clearly doesn't understand what it means, and funniest because she's defending it even as the outcome was obviously terrible. The escalation to 'I leaned in! I don't know, David' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We should probably get Gwen out of the room.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'll kill her.

jokes.text: Everybody, I'd like to introduce Alexis, she's going to be joining us tonight. So that way we'll all have partners. / That's great! / Dot, you won't need the Swiffer.
jokes.setup: Ted introduces Alexis to the seniors as a new dance partner.
jokes.punchline: 'Dot, you won't need the Swiffer' — Dot has apparently been using a Swiffer mop as a dance partner, which is both incredibly sad and very funny.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single-line jokes. 'Dot, you won't need the Swiffer' implies an entire backstory and situation (Dot using a Swiffer as a substitute dance partner) without ever showing it. Maximum economy — the image is conjured in five words and it's equal parts funny and poignant. High originality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm lactose intolerant.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm gluten-free.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And I don't eat anything that casts a shadow.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, this is gonna be fun!

jokes.text: I guess I'll be dancing with Ted. / I thought Carol assigns the dance partners. / We'll all get a chance to dance with Ted.
jokes.setup: Alexis is introduced and immediately a senior volunteer claims Ted.
jokes.punchline: The established protocol of 'Carol assigns partners' and Ted's diplomatic 'we'll all get a chance to dance with Ted' reveals this is a known territorial battle among the seniors.
jokes.explanation: Good worldbuilding joke — implies a whole social hierarchy at the seniors' dance class. Ted referring to himself in the third person ('get a chance to dance with Ted') is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland, you can't drive.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because you don't have a license.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I have a license.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Not a valid one. Also, you've been drinking.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you're drunk, Roland. You're very drunk.

jokes.text: Although I did think that there would be more of them. / Yeah, well there used to be, but... you know.
jokes.setup: Alexis makes a comment about the small number of seniors at the dance class.
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'Yeah, well there used to be, but... you know' — trailing off to imply the obvious (they died) with maximum understatement.
jokes.explanation: The trailing '...you know' to indicate death by attrition is a beautifully dark understatement. Maximum economy — the unspoken word is funnier than saying it would be. Strong dark comedy that earns a surprised laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: They're quite haunting, actually.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sure Moira would be thrilled to hear that her photos are haunting. That's exactly the vibe she's going for.

jokes.text: Oh, okay, but these ones actually smell like very clean, like baby powder. / Yeah, I think it's probably better if you call the girls by their names.
jokes.setup: Alexis had previously said she has 'a thing with old smells.'
jokes.punchline: She conducts an olfactory assessment of the seniors ('they smell like baby powder') and Ted has to redirect her to use their names instead.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the smell concern, and Alexis's clinical assessment (they pass the smell test) is a funny beat. Ted's correction is deadpan and earned.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: David, I want to apologize. I realize that my actions may have triggered some unresolved trauma patterns within your psyche, and I'm here to hold space for your feelings while also acknowledging my own emotional journey through this experience.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not an apology.

dialogue.speaker: Sebastien
dialogue.line: I'm hearing that you're not ready to receive my vulnerable authenticity right now, and I respect that boundary.

jokes.text: Why would he bring her here? / Are you talking about me?
jokes.setup: A senior is clearly talking about Alexis's presence as an outsider threatening the Ted dynamic.
jokes.punchline: Alexis innocently asks if they're talking about her — she's close enough to hear, making it maximally awkward.
jokes.explanation: A functional cringe beat. Not exceptional but serves the scene's social awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Alexis has had quite the journey with engagements, hasn't she?

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Yes, well, at this point I've lost count.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's practically a hobby.

jokes.text: Does Dot have arthritis? Because it looks like she's giving me the finger. / Well, she has arthritis, / but not in that hand.
jokes.setup: Alexis notices Dot's hand gesture during dancing.
jokes.punchline: Ted's two-beat response: first confirming the arthritis (suggesting innocence), then 'but not in that hand' — confirming Dot is deliberately giving Alexis the finger.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. Structure is perfect: the setup creates a charitable interpretation (arthritis = involuntary gesture), the punchline destroys it with surgical precision ('but not in that hand'). It's a two-beat misdirection that fires perfectly. The visual of a sweet elderly woman deliberately flipping off Alexis is both dark and genuinely hilarious. Very quotable, high rewatch value, and the delivery split across a scene marker makes it even better. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor and genuinely exceeds it in structural elegance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So what's going on with Ted and your granddaughter?

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Well, he's not interested.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Because he's gay, dear.

jokes.text: Full house. Read 'em and weep. / And I think I come in second because I got a pair of Queens. / There's no second place, Roland.
jokes.setup: Bob wins at poker with a full house.
jokes.punchline: Roland claims second place with 'a pair of queens' — not understanding the game at all — and Johnny has to explain there is no second place.
jokes.explanation: A clean character joke that sets up the revelation later that Roland doesn't know the rules. 'I come in second' for poker is a funny misunderstanding, especially from someone who's ostensibly playing regularly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We're just friends, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Well, that's what your father and I have, and we've been married for forty years.

jokes.text: Yeah, I guess it's another trip to Bob's laundromat, right? You know, because he keeps taking us to the cleaners.
jokes.setup: Bob keeps winning at poker.
jokes.punchline: Roland's labored pun — 'Bob's laundromat... taking us to the cleaners' — is delivered with the pride of someone who thinks he's made a very clever observation.
jokes.explanation: The pun is deliberately bad, which IS the joke — Roland's self-satisfaction about a weak wordplay is character-consistent. But the humor comes from the meta-level (Roland thinks this is good) rather than the pun itself. Functional but weak.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Do you want to dance with me?

dialogue.speaker: Dot
dialogue.line: I'd rather spend the evening with my mop and bucket.

jokes.text: Haven't you noticed how Gwen circles the table with appetizers, constantly eying Bob. Then he lays down a big fat bet and wins.
jokes.setup: Johnny suspects Bob and Gwen are cheating at poker.
jokes.punchline: Johnny has constructed an entire cheating conspiracy around Gwen circling with appetizers as a signaling system.
jokes.explanation: The conspiracy theory is plausible enough to be funny — it sounds absurd but the logic holds up just enough. Works as a setup for the later reveal that Bob just reads tells.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I have a theory that all men cheat.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I don't cheat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's what they all say.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: No, I really don't. I don't need to cheat. I'm married to my best friend.

jokes.text: You know, come to think of it, those matching visors they're a little suspicious. / I don't want to believe this, But ah, I do miss my wristwatch.
jokes.setup: Johnny suspects Bob and Gwen of cheating; Roland is joining the theory.
jokes.punchline: Roland adds 'matching visors' as suspicious evidence, then reveals he's already lost a wristwatch to Bob — suggesting a history of losing that he'd previously suppressed.
jokes.explanation: 'I do miss my wristwatch' is a good escalating revelation — Roland's willingness to believe the conspiracy is fueled by genuine loss. The matching visors as evidence is also funny as a logical leap.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Roland, your tell is that you always fold when you have a good hand.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's because I don't know the rules.

jokes.text: Ahhh. Like if she had an 'accident' or something?
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests they test Bob's luck when Gwen is not around.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately interprets 'not around' as potentially fatal — his mind goes to 'accident' before 'go to the store.'
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy beat — Roland's casual escalation to implied murder as a solution reveals a darkly comic worldview. The air-quotes around 'accident' are a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Sebastien, darling, I appreciate your passion, but what you're describing isn't artistic vision—it's gaslighting with a French accent.

jokes.text: I don't mean bump her off, Roland.
jokes.setup: Roland has suggested Gwen might have an 'accident.'
jokes.punchline: Johnny has to explicitly clarify he doesn't mean murder, which is funny because this needs clarifying.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Johnny needs to say this at all. Good reaction-beat structure, economical.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have Sebastien's memory card. All of his photos are on here.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's great! What happened to the originals?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the first copy got destroyed when I spilled wine on my laptop.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The second copy was in a folder that blew out of my car on the highway.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And the third copy was... I may have sat on it.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You sat on it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was in a chair. I didn't know it was there.

jokes.text: Gwen's gotta go!
jokes.setup: Johnny was about to diplomatically suggest Gwen might go to the store.
jokes.punchline: Roland blurts out 'Gwen's gotta go!' — an abrupt, unsubtle declaration that undermines the careful approach Johnny was attempting.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between Johnny's careful diplomacy and Roland's detonation of the plan with three words. The bluntness is funny, especially after Roland volunteered to be the subtle one.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you for protecting me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I got to destroy my ex and help my mother. It's a win-win.

jokes.text: Kosher? / And halal. / Gwen, you called it!
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to explain why Gwen needs to go to the store; he says the food needs 'something a little more...' and can't think of a reason.
jokes.punchline: Roland says 'Kosher'; Johnny adds 'And halal'; Bob cheerfully validates this with 'Gwen, you called it!' — as if Gwen had just predicted this dietary restriction need.
jokes.explanation: The improvisational escalation (Kosher → Halal → gluten-free) with Bob's misunderstanding that Gwen 'called it' is a funny absurdist sequence. The specificity of 'Gwen, you called it' — Bob thinking his wife predicted this — is particularly good.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, is that my watch?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, you lost it to me in poker last week. You don't remember?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I... no. How much was I down?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: About three hundred. You said 'I'll put my watch against a hundred bucks.'

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, that's a vintage Rolex.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you shouldn't have bet it then.

jokes.text: Oh Gosh, I'm sorry I didn't think of this. But you know, maybe... maybe Roland could go? / He's running pretty low on chips. / Sure. I can drive. / I don't think you want to drive, Roland. / I don't mind. I love driving.
jokes.setup: The group is trying to send Gwen to the store but Bob suggests Roland go instead.
jokes.punchline: Roland enthusiastically volunteers to drive despite signals from Johnny; the scene builds to Roland admitting he doesn't love driving at night and is drunk.
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation sequence building toward the drunk admission. Each beat adds a complication. Gets better as it goes.

jokes.text: Yeah, I don't love driving. Not at night. No! / And I'm drunk.
jokes.setup: Roland has insisted he loves driving; Johnny pushes back.
jokes.punchline: Roland walks back 'I love driving' to 'I don't love driving. Not at night.' and then adds the punchline: 'And I'm drunk.'
jokes.explanation: The structure is very clean: escalating retraction ('I love driving' → 'not at night' → 'and I'm drunk'). The drunk revelation lands as a punchline because the other qualifications had already softened us up. Roland's delivery of this as if it's a reasonable final factor is very much his character.

jokes.text: The photos are really haunting. / Haunting. / Sure she'd be thrilled about that adjective.
jokes.setup: Sebastien describes Moira's photos as 'haunting.'
jokes.punchline: David's dry observation — 'Sure she'd be thrilled about that adjective' — captures Moira's vanity perfectly; 'haunting' is not a compliment Moira would want.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly calibrated joke — David repeating 'haunting' deadpan, then noting Moira's likely displeasure, rewards knowledge of the character. It's also a subtle dig at Sebastien (his 'art' makes people look haunted). High character integration because this requires knowing both David's wit and Moira's vanity.

jokes.text: And while my therapist said I should never feel sorrow, I do appreciate your pain.
jokes.setup: Sebastien is addressing the pain caused by their breakup.
jokes.punchline: His therapist has told him to 'never feel sorrow' — a baffling therapeutic goal that Sebastien reports with complete sincerity — and he squares it with offering to 'appreciate' David's pain instead.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurd. The idea that a therapist told Sebastien to never feel sorrow is funny on its own, but his uncritical acceptance and deployment of this as an excuse to avoid empathy is perfect character comedy. 'I do appreciate your pain' as a substitute for remorse is a sublime piece of narcissist-speak. High originality.

jokes.text: Oh... / Well, I shouldn't. / Oh you should. / Okay, fine, but um, I can't stay.
jokes.setup: Sebastien propositions David.
jokes.punchline: David's series of increasingly less convincing objections ('Oh... Well, I shouldn't. / Okay, fine, but I can't stay') collapse almost instantly — he capitulates to someone he's described as a monster.
jokes.explanation: David's capitulation arc — 'I shouldn't' → 'Okay, fine' in seconds — is a perfect comic deflation of his earlier moral posturing. The 'I can't stay' condition is a hilarious token gesture of resistance. Character-specific comedy that rewards David-watchers.

jokes.text: Oh well... we're not dating. / But I do understand that old women can get very possessive about young... younger...
jokes.setup: Joan tells Alexis the ladies have grown fond of Ted.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's dismissal starts as 'young' then self-corrects to 'younger' — revealing she was about to call Ted young, noticed that makes her sound older, and pivoted mid-word.
jokes.explanation: The mid-word self-correction from 'young' to 'younger' is a subtle but precise comedy beat — Alexis catching herself implying she's old in the same breath as dismissing the seniors. High character integration; this is very specifically Alexis.

jokes.text: Dear, we know all about the failed engagements. But don't worry, I'm not going to rake you over the coals to find out how on earth you ever let that one get away. Or why?
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to maintain that she and Ted are just friends.
jokes.punchline: Joan says she won't 'rake Alexis over the coals' — then immediately begins doing exactly that, cataloguing her failed engagements and demanding to know why she let Ted go.
jokes.explanation: Joan's self-contradiction — stating she won't interrogate Alexis while interrogating her — is a well-observed joke about a certain type of person who uses disclaimers to enable exactly what they're disclaiming. The 'Or why?' after 'how on earth you let that one get away' is a nice double barrel.

jokes.text: Okay... / It's none of my business and I'm sure you have your reasons. / I'd just want to know what they are?
jokes.setup: Joan says it's none of her business why Alexis let Ted get away.
jokes.punchline: She immediately adds 'I'd just want to know what they are?' — following 'none of my business' with active pursuit of that information.
jokes.explanation: The escalating self-contradiction of Joan is well-paced. 'None of my business... I'd just want to know what they are' is a good irony beat that extends the joke from the previous setup.

jokes.text: Is she not cute? / She's not you.
jokes.setup: Joan mentions she's been trying to set Ted up with her granddaughter for two years.
jokes.punchline: Alexis asks if the granddaughter isn't cute; Joan's 'She's not you' is a backhanded non-answer that redirects entirely to the Alexis/Ted situation.
jokes.explanation: 'She's not you' is economical and cuts right to the heart of the scene — Joan doesn't bother engaging with the granddaughter's merits, she just points at the obvious answer. Works both as comedy and as an emotionally true beat for Alexis.

jokes.text: I think you ladies are just a little bit confused. Ted and I are just very close friends. / Hm. / My husband and I were really close friends for 52 years.
jokes.setup: Alexis deflects by insisting she and Ted are 'just close friends.'
jokes.punchline: Joan's 'My husband and I were really close friends for 52 years' — reframing 'just friends' as a description of her marriage, implicitly endorsing the Ted relationship.
jokes.explanation: Excellent construction. Alexis means 'just friends = nothing romantic,' Joan redefines 'close friends' as a perfect description of a 52-year marriage. The reframe is both funny and genuinely moving — it's the show's warm humor at its best. The economy is superb; Joan doesn't belabor it. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Mind if I cut in? / Oh, yeah. / But I'm also happy to keep dancing with one of them. / No, I meant I could dance with Joan. / Just the ladies get pretty upset if I don't divide my time between them.
jokes.setup: Ted asks to cut in on Alexis's dance.
jokes.punchline: Alexis assumes he's cutting in to dance with her; Ted clarifies he meant Joan. Then he explains he has to carefully distribute his attention among all the seniors like a diplomat.
jokes.explanation: A good misdirection — the assumed interpretation (Ted wants to dance with Alexis romantically) is flipped (he wants Joan, for senior diplomacy). Ted's careful management of elderly feelings is a sweet character reveal.

jokes.text: Well then I will dance with Dot! / I would rather dance with the Swiffer.
jokes.setup: Alexis is left without a partner and volunteers to dance with Dot.
jokes.punchline: Dot prefers the Swiffer to Alexis — the earlier Swiffer-as-dance-partner is now weaponized as a specific rejection of Alexis.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's second-best joke. Dot — who has also been giving Alexis the finger — explicitly states she prefers her Swiffer dance partner over Alexis. The callback to the Swiffer introduction makes this land twice as hard. The economy is perfect: 'I would rather dance with the Swiffer' is a devastating, quotable line. Dot has become the episode's secret MVP.

jokes.text: Well, I guess it wasn't Gwen, was it, Johnny? / [long pause] / Sorry? / [another pause] / Nothing, nothing, Bob.
jokes.setup: Bob wins another hand without Gwen present, exposing Johnny's cheating theory as wrong.
jokes.punchline: Bob knows Johnny suspected him; the extended awkward pause before 'Sorry?' and then Roland's betrayal ('Johnny had this theory that...') collapses the situation.
jokes.explanation: The uncomfortable pause and Roland immediately throwing Johnny under the bus is a good cringe sequence. The awkward silence is structural comedy.

jokes.text: Well, Uh... / Johnny, why don't you tell Bob your theory. / [pause]
jokes.setup: Johnny is squirming as Bob confronts him about the cheating theory.
jokes.punchline: Roland, instead of covering for Johnny, cheerfully invites Johnny to explain the theory himself.
jokes.explanation: Roland's cheerful betrayal of Johnny is a consistent character beat — he means no harm but consistently makes things worse. The pause after the invitation is the visual comedy.

jokes.text: No offense, but I don't need to cheat to beat any of you.
jokes.setup: Johnny confesses the cheating suspicion to Bob.
jokes.punchline: Bob's calm, cutting response — 'I don't need to cheat to beat any of you' — is delivered without malice but is a comprehensive dismissal.
jokes.explanation: Bob's quiet confidence is well-deployed here. The 'no offense' preceding an extremely offensive truth is a classic understated comedy beat.

jokes.text: Listen now Ronnie, when you - when you have a good hand, you close your cards real quick. And Ray, when you've got a bad hand, you start tapping your foot. And Johnny your right eyebrow kind of just pops up. And Roland... well, you know...
jokes.setup: Bob explains he reads their tells rather than cheating.
jokes.punchline: Each character gets a specific tell, escalating to Roland — for whom Bob can't even articulate a coherent tell, trailing off with 'well, you know...' implying Roland's entire demeanor is a tell.
jokes.explanation: The escalating tell-reveal is very well-structured, with each character getting a specific, believable tell. The payoff — 'And Roland... well, you know...' — is the punchline, implying Roland is so obviously readable that it defies description. The structure (specific → specific → specific → vague dismissal) is a classic comedic escalation that ends on the best possible note. High character integration for Roland specifically.

jokes.text: I don't know all of them. No.
jokes.setup: Bob implies Roland doesn't know the rules of poker.
jokes.punchline: Roland's candid admission — 'I don't know all of them. No.' — confirms he has been playing without full knowledge of the rules.
jokes.explanation: The admission pays off the earlier joke about Roland claiming second place. 'I don't know all of them. No.' is a perfect Roland line — completely without shame or embarrassment, just honest. Very economical and well-placed in the sequence.

jokes.text: Truth is we put most of the winnings towards the snacks that Gwen serves up. We thought it was kind of a special evening for everyone.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland have been convinced Bob and Gwen are cheating
jokes.punchline: Bob reveals Gwen's appetizers are paid for by the poker winnings — he's been hosting a community dinner, essentially, through their losses
jokes.explanation: A warm-comedy subversion of the cheating premise — the 'scheme' was actually hospitality. The irony that the conspiracy theorists were eating the proceeds of their own losses is delicious. Good Schitt's Creek warmth without being saccharine.

jokes.text: Well you know, truth is we put most of the winnings towards the snacks that Gwen serves up. We thought it was kind of a special evening for everyone.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland have been convinced Bob is a cheating shark who cleans them out.
jokes.punchline: Bob reveals the poker winnings go back into the snacks Gwen prepares — the entire 'taking us to the cleaners' dynamic was actually a community-funded dinner.
jokes.explanation: The reveal is a satisfying misdirection payoff — all the conspiratorial build-up about Gwen and Bob cheating resolves into wholesome community snack-funding. The contrast between the paranoia and the innocent reality is the joke. Works because it also makes Johnny and Roland look foolish, which is earned.

jokes.text: In my defense, I didn't believe any of this, Bob. Gwen is very trustworthy. / Ray, you're tapping your foot. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Roland tries to distance himself from the cheating theory he enthusiastically supported.
jokes.punchline: Bob immediately catches Ray's tell — tapping his foot — in real time, confirming his poker-reading ability while Roland's protest is ignored.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully efficient callback — Bob demonstrates his tell-reading in real time while Roland's attempted betrayal of Johnny is completely ignored. The simultaneous confirmation of Bob's skill and Roland's faithlessness lands as a strong button on the poker storyline.

jokes.text: I was up all night evaluating our little project and I've come to the unfortunate conclusion that you may have misrepresented your intentions.
jokes.setup: Moira confronts Sebastien about exploiting her for photos.
jokes.punchline: 'You may have misrepresented your intentions' — Moira's ultra-formal, diplomatic phrasing for being taken advantage of.
jokes.explanation: Moira's characteristic oblique phrasing turns a confrontation into a formal diplomatic complaint. The understatement of 'misrepresented your intentions' for what was clearly exploitation is very Moira.

jokes.text: Oh, I'd love to believe you, but there's a big fat line between charm and bullshit.
jokes.setup: Sebastien insists she will 'sit atop the summit of cultural conversation' with his work.
jokes.punchline: 'There's a big fat line between charm and bullshit' — Moira momentarily cuts through all her own pretension with devastating clarity.
jokes.explanation: This is Moira at her best — sharp, self-aware, and funny. The line itself is quotable. 'Big fat line between charm and bullshit' has the cadence of a bon mot and character integration is high because Moira using the word 'bullshit' with such precision signals she's not playing around.

jokes.text: Well, I'm having a hard time landing on surprise or betrayal.
jokes.setup: Moira discovers David has come from Sebastien's room.
jokes.punchline: Her precise articulation of being unable to choose between surprise and betrayal — rather than simply feeling both — is quintessentially Moira.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira line — she can't just feel an emotion, she has to catalog and choose between them with bureaucratic precision. 'Having a hard time landing on surprise or betrayal' is very quotable and entirely specific to her character.

jokes.text: Oh, you mean this memory card? Yeah, I'd give it back, but I don't think it'll be of much use to you. It accidentally fell out of your camera into my hand last night. And then fell out of my hand into my drink. And then I stepped on it a lot. So...
jokes.setup: Moira demands Sebastien's memory card with her photos; David has it.
jokes.punchline: David's escalating account of the 'accidents' that befell the memory card — 'fell into my hand... fell into my drink... stepped on it a lot' — is a brilliantly deadpan narration of deliberate sabotage described as misfortune.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments and among the best David Rose jokes in the series at this point. The escalating 'accident' sequence — each step making it clearer it was deliberate — is perfectly constructed. The cumulative absurdity of 'fell into my hand' → 'fell into my drink' → 'stepped on it a lot' (the 'a lot' is the gem) lands as both funny and unexpectedly sweet, because it reveals David protected Moira despite his own complicated feelings. 'So...' as the trailing-off button is immaculate. Deserves the high score.

jokes.text: David, I feel terrible that you had to do that just for me. / It wasn't just for you. Let's just say we both won.
jokes.setup: Moira is moved that David destroyed the memory card for her.
jokes.punchline: David's 'It wasn't just for you. Let's just say we both won.' — implying he got something out of his overnight with Sebastien, turning a warm moment into a self-serving admission.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully calibrated David line — it undercuts the sentimental moment while also being genuinely affectionate. 'Let's just say we both won' is the perfect amount of ambiguity. The humor is in David maintaining his ironic detachment even in a genuine moment. Very high character integration — this is quintessentially David.

jokes.text: The game is called crazy eights. And the diamond shaped things are wild. / Roland, shush.
jokes.setup: Poker night resumes after the Bob revelation.
jokes.punchline: Roland announces the game's rules to the table as if this is new information — revealing he truly doesn't know the rules and thinks 'the diamond shaped things' are called that.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that confirms Roland doesn't know the rules — 'the diamond shaped things are wild' not only shows his ignorance but his odd, earnest way of communicating it. 'Roland, shush' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Those aren't for you.
jokes.setup: Roland is eyeing the teriyaki meatballs Gwen prepared.
jokes.punchline: Bob's flat 'Those aren't for you' — a territorial, calm assertion about the snacks — implies ongoing scores to settle.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — 'Those aren't for you' does everything. Bob's residual displeasure with Roland is communicated in four words without any dramatization.

jokes.text: How's that watch working out for you? / I love it. / Thanks.
jokes.setup: Bob has recovered Roland's wristwatch from his winnings.
jokes.punchline: Johnny asks how the watch is; Roland says 'I love it' and — crucially — 'Thanks,' implying he's thanking the watch or the situation, and this is simply the end of that subplot.
jokes.explanation: A warm callback that closes the poker storyline. Not a big laugh but a satisfying comedic button on the wristwatch thread.

tmdb_overview: David’s photographer ex-boyfriend arrives in town to do a photo series on Moira. Johnny and Roland suspect that Bob is cheating at poker.
insight: Sebastien Raine episode hits 84.4 with 2.48 jokes per minute sustained across 54 gags.
